item.title: Cross-Scenario Deraining Adaptation with Unpaired Data: Superpixel Structural Priors and Multi-Stage Pseudo-Rain Synthesize
item.dc:creator: Kangbo Zhao
Miaoxin Guan
Xiang Chen
Yukai Shi
Jinshan Pan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Image deraining plays a pivotal role in low-level computer vision, serving as a prerequisite for robust outdoor surveillance and autonomous driving systems. While deep learning paradigms have achieved remarkable success in firmly aligned settings, they often suffer from severe performance degradation when generalized to unseen Out-of-Distribution (OOD) scenarios. This failure stems primarily from the significant domain discrepancy between synthetic training datasets and the complex physical dynamics of real-world rain. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a pioneering cross-scenario deraining adaptation framework. Diverging from conventional approaches, our method obviates the requirements for paired rainy observations in the target domain, leveraging exclusively rain-free background images. We design a Superpixel Generation (Sup-Gen) module to extract stable structural priors from the source domain using Simple Linear Iterative Clustering. Subsequently, a Resolution-adaptive Fusion strategy is introduced to align these source structures with target backgrounds through texture similarity, ensuring the synthesis of diverse and realistic pseudo-data. Finally, we implement a pseudo-label re-Synthesize mechanism that employs multi-stage noise generation to simulate realistic rain streaks. This framework functions as a versatile plug-and-play module capable of seamless integration into arbitrary deraining architectures. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models demonstrate that our approach yields remarkable PSNR gains of up to 32% to 59% in OOD domains while significantly accelerating training convergence. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kangbo Zhao; Miaoxin Guan; Xiang Chen; Yukai Shi; Jinshan Pan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700727

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Image deraining plays a pivotal role in low-level computer vision, serving as a prerequisite for robust outdoor surveillance and autonomous driving systems. While deep learning paradigms have achieved remarkable success in firmly aligned settings, they often suffer from severe performance degradation when generalized to unseen Out-of-Distribution (OOD) scenarios. This failure stems primarily from the significant domain discrepancy between synthetic training datasets and the complex physical dynamics of real-world rain. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a pioneering cross-scenario deraining adaptation framework. Diverging from conventional approaches, our method obviates the requirements for paired rainy observations in the target domain, leveraging exclusively rain-free background images. We design a Superpixel Generation (Sup-Gen) module to extract stable structural priors from the source domain using Simple Linear Iterative Clustering. Subsequently, a Resolution-adaptive Fusion strategy is introduced to align these source structures with target backgrounds through texture similarity, ensuring the synthesis of diverse and realistic pseudo-data. Finally, we implement a pseudo-label re-Synthesize mechanism that employs multi-stage noise generation to simulate realistic rain streaks. This framework functions as a versatile plug-and-play module capable of seamless integration into arbitrary deraining architectures. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models demonstrate that our approach yields remarkable PSNR gains of up to 32% to 59% in OOD domains while significantly accelerating training convergence.

item.title: Optical-Guided Neural Collapse for SAR Few-Shot Class Incremental Learning
item.dc:creator: Fan Zhang
Sijin Zheng
Fei Ma
Qiang Yin
Yongsheng Zhou
Fei Gao
Xian Sun
item.description: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in synthetic aperture radar imagery presents unique challenges due to severe data scarcity and SAR-specific variability. In particular, strong azimuth sensitivity in SAR induces large intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and FSCIL sequential updates further lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned classes. Inspired by neural collapse, we propose an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework, which derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset and uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning. SAR features are projected onto these orthogonal subspaces via principal angle constraints, effectively transferring discriminative structure from the optical to the SAR domain. Specifically, our projection loss and the classifier loss optimized with a frozen simplex-ETF geometry jointly induce neural collapse by concentrating features around class means while maintaining large inter-class angles. We evaluate the approach on a benchmark comprising an optical ATR dataset and a SAR ATR dataset with 24 target classes, organized into a base training session and seven incremental sessions. Compared with recent FSCIL methods including NCFSCIL and so on, our method achieves the highest final accuracy and a favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation. Moreover, neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, indicating that the learned features more closely approximate the ideal simplex-ETF geometry. Published: 2026-06-03T07:09:20+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fan Zhang; Sijin Zheng; Fei Ma; Qiang Yin; Yongsheng Zhou; Fei Gao; Xian Sun

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in synthetic aperture radar imagery presents unique challenges due to severe data scarcity and SAR-specific variability. In particular, strong azimuth sensitivity in SAR induces large intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and FSCIL sequential updates further lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned classes. Inspired by neural collapse, we propose an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework, which derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset and uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning. SAR features are projected onto these orthogonal subspaces via principal angle constraints, effectively transferring discriminative structure from the optical to the SAR domain. Specifically, our projection loss and the classifier loss optimized with a frozen simplex-ETF geometry jointly induce neural collapse by concentrating features around class means while maintaining large inter-class angles. We evaluate the approach on a benchmark comprising an optical ATR dataset and a SAR ATR dataset with 24 target classes, organized into a base training session and seven incremental sessions. Compared with recent FSCIL methods including NCFSCIL and so on, our method achieves the highest final accuracy and a favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation. Moreover, neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, indicating that the learned features more closely approximate the ideal simplex-ETF geometry.

item.title: BiCPF: Bi-Conditional Parallel-Fusion Network for Composed Image Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Yong Luo
Jinrong Yang
Xingyu Yang
Daqing Liu
Minjing Dong
Mengya Han
Zhiwei Ye
Liang Huang
Dongjing Shan
item.description: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) aims to retrieve a target image given a reference image and a modification text. However, existing fusion-based methods often produce modality dominant queries that consistently lean toward a certain modality, thereby impairing the model’s ability to effectively handle diverse real-world scenarios. In contrast, humans performing composed image retrieval dynamically reinterpret visual content from the perspective of text while simultaneously grounding language understanding in visual context, forming a bidirectional cross-modal semantic construction process. Motivated by this, we propose a Bi-Conditional Parallel-Fusion Network ( BiCPF ) that models cross-modal semantic relations from two complementary perspectives: an image-conditioned branch interprets textual modifications within the visual context, while a text-conditioned branch reinterprets visual semantics guided by textual cues. Specifically, our BiCPF includes: 1) Bi-Conditional Parallel-Fusion Module that utilizes two parallel fusion layers to learn modality-grounded queries conditioned on the complementary modality; 2) Similarity Aggregation Module that dynamically balances the contributions of textual and visual similarities for adaptive retrieval to handle diverse retrieval scenarios. Extensive experiments conducted on three benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in scenarios where treating one modality as dominance is insufficient for accurate retrieval. Published: 2026-06-05T16:25:07+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yong Luo; Jinrong Yang; Xingyu Yang; Daqing Liu; Minjing Dong; Mengya Han; Zhiwei Ye; Liang Huang; Dongjing Shan

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104514

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) aims to retrieve a target image given a reference image and a modification text. However, existing fusion-based methods often produce modality dominant queries that consistently lean toward a certain modality, thereby impairing the model’s ability to effectively handle diverse real-world scenarios. In contrast, humans performing composed image retrieval dynamically reinterpret visual content from the perspective of text while simultaneously grounding language understanding in visual context, forming a bidirectional cross-modal semantic construction process. Motivated by this, we propose a Bi-Conditional Parallel-Fusion Network ( BiCPF ) that models cross-modal semantic relations from two complementary perspectives: an image-conditioned branch interprets textual modifications within the visual context, while a text-conditioned branch reinterprets visual semantics guided by textual cues. Specifically, our BiCPF includes: 1) Bi-Conditional Parallel-Fusion Module that utilizes two parallel fusion layers to learn modality-grounded queries conditioned on the complementary modality; 2) Similarity Aggregation Module that dynamically balances the contributions of textual and visual similarities for adaptive retrieval to handle diverse retrieval scenarios. Extensive experiments conducted on three benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in scenarios where treating one modality as dominance is insufficient for accurate retrieval.

item.title: Explicit location-label-guided foreground feature optimization learning for few-shot classification
item.dc:creator: Bin Song
Hong Zhu
Bingxin Wang
Yuandong Bi
item.description: In few-shot image classification, spurious correlations between background and categories diminish foreground feature saliency and constrain cross-category representation. Introducing foreground location annotations helps models distinguish foreground from background, thereby enhancing foreground feature saliency. However, existing methods lack explicit location-label-guided optimization mechanisms. To address this gap, we propose Classification BY Detection (CBYD), a novel paradigm that reformulates few-shot classification within an object detection framework to leverage spatial supervision for enhancing foreground feature saliency. Specifically, 1) we devise an automatic annotation method to expand classification datasets with foreground bounding boxes; 2) we incorporate a gradient decoupling algorithm into the object detection network to resolve optimization conflicts among branches under few-shot constraints; 3) we propose a multi-object comprehensive decision algorithm to align detection outputs with classification requirements. Extensive experiments on two coarse-grained datasets (mini-ImageNet and tiered-ImageNet) and three fine-grained datasets (CUB, Stanford Dogs, and Stanford Cars) demonstrate that our method improves performance. Notably, on mini-ImageNet, CBYD++ attains 85.63% and 94.80% accuracy for 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot tasks, respectively; on tiered-ImageNet, it achieves 90.13% and 95.67%. These results validate that explicit location-label guidance effectively enhances foreground feature saliency and cross-category representation in few-shot classification. Published: 2026-06-04T16:19:12+00:00 Venue: Information Sciences Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bin Song; Hong Zhu; Bingxin Wang; Yuandong Bi

Journal: Information Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.ins.2026.123744

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

In few-shot image classification, spurious correlations between background and categories diminish foreground feature saliency and constrain cross-category representation. Introducing foreground location annotations helps models distinguish foreground from background, thereby enhancing foreground feature saliency. However, existing methods lack explicit location-label-guided optimization mechanisms. To address this gap, we propose Classification BY Detection (CBYD), a novel paradigm that reformulates few-shot classification within an object detection framework to leverage spatial supervision for enhancing foreground feature saliency. Specifically, 1) we devise an automatic annotation method to expand classification datasets with foreground bounding boxes; 2) we incorporate a gradient decoupling algorithm into the object detection network to resolve optimization conflicts among branches under few-shot constraints; 3) we propose a multi-object comprehensive decision algorithm to align detection outputs with classification requirements. Extensive experiments on two coarse-grained datasets (mini-ImageNet and tiered-ImageNet) and three fine-grained datasets (CUB, Stanford Dogs, and Stanford Cars) demonstrate that our method improves performance. Notably, on mini-ImageNet, CBYD++ attains 85.63% and 94.80% accuracy for 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot tasks, respectively; on tiered-ImageNet, it achieves 90.13% and 95.67%. These results validate that explicit location-label guidance effectively enhances foreground feature saliency and cross-category representation in few-shot classification.

item.title: MonoHPE: Monocular 3D Object Detection with Height Positional Encoding
item.dc:creator: Chenxi Zhu
Zhi Li
Zhenyu Dai
Long Zheng
Weidong Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Monocular 3D object detection in autonomous driving has achieved revolutionary breakthroughs, primarily attributed to integrating perspective projection and geometric priors. Despite the continuous surge in overall performance, we argue that the subtle yet critical challenge of insufficient geometric constraints in the height dimension has not been sufficiently dissected, resulting in a systematic deviation between the bounding box height and the actual projected height, thereby nonlinearly amplifying the error in depth estimation. To uncover the profound essence of this error amplification mechanism, this paper employs a differentiable analytical framework to dissect the ratio dynamics of depth perception in perspective projection, providing a principled explanation of the sensitivity of depth perception to height deviations. Inspired by these insights, we propose MonoHPE, a method based on a hierarchical task learning paradigm. This method explicitly strengthens the geometric associations in the height dimension through an ingenious position modeling mechanism, achieving a breakthrough leap in the precision of depth estimation. Furthermore, to enhance the model’s adaptability in complex environments, we innovatively introduce an adaptive activation-guided loss, which directs the network to focus on semantically dominant regions, effectively suppressing foreground interference and noise contamination. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI dataset. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenxi Zhu; Zhi Li; Zhenyu Dai; Long Zheng; Weidong Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700175

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Monocular 3D object detection in autonomous driving has achieved revolutionary breakthroughs, primarily attributed to integrating perspective projection and geometric priors. Despite the continuous surge in overall performance, we argue that the subtle yet critical challenge of insufficient geometric constraints in the height dimension has not been sufficiently dissected, resulting in a systematic deviation between the bounding box height and the actual projected height, thereby nonlinearly amplifying the error in depth estimation. To uncover the profound essence of this error amplification mechanism, this paper employs a differentiable analytical framework to dissect the ratio dynamics of depth perception in perspective projection, providing a principled explanation of the sensitivity of depth perception to height deviations. Inspired by these insights, we propose MonoHPE, a method based on a hierarchical task learning paradigm. This method explicitly strengthens the geometric associations in the height dimension through an ingenious position modeling mechanism, achieving a breakthrough leap in the precision of depth estimation. Furthermore, to enhance the model’s adaptability in complex environments, we innovatively introduce an adaptive activation-guided loss, which directs the network to focus on semantically dominant regions, effectively suppressing foreground interference and noise contamination. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI dataset.

item.title: $A^2$: Smaller Self-Supervised ViTs Localize Better than Larger Ones
item.dc:creator: Sreehari Rammohan
Huy Ha
Carl Vondrick
item.description: Robust visual classification often depends on localizing the main foreground objects in an image while ignoring contextual distractors. Surprisingly, we find that the attention maps of smaller self-supervised ViTs localize foreground objects better than those of larger ViTs. However, we still need large ViTs, because they extract richer representations from each patch. To get the best of both worlds, good localization and rich representations, we propose $A^2$, a simple method that leverages this inverse scaling finding by decoupling where to look (a small attention model) from what to extract (a large embedding model): we crop around the attention peaks of a small model and embed the crops with a larger model. $A^2$ uses entirely pretrained features, requires no group labels, and does not require per-dataset attention or backbone training. Across 5 benchmarks, $A^2$ is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods like DFR, and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts. Published: 2026-06-02T04:45:02+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sreehari Rammohan; Huy Ha; Carl Vondrick

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Robust visual classification often depends on localizing the main foreground objects in an image while ignoring contextual distractors. Surprisingly, we find that the attention maps of smaller self-supervised ViTs localize foreground objects better than those of larger ViTs. However, we still need large ViTs, because they extract richer representations from each patch. To get the best of both worlds, good localization and rich representations, we propose $A^2$, a simple method that leverages this inverse scaling finding by decoupling where to look (a small attention model) from what to extract (a large embedding model): we crop around the attention peaks of a small model and embed the crops with a larger model. $A^2$ uses entirely pretrained features, requires no group labels, and does not require per-dataset attention or backbone training. Across 5 benchmarks, $A^2$ is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods like DFR, and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts.

item.title: Detail++: Training-Free Detail Enhancer for T2I Diffusion Models
item.dc:creator: Lifeng Chen
Jiner Wang
Zihao Pan
Beier Zhu
Xiaofeng Yang
Chi Zhang
item.description: Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have led to impressive visual results. However, these models still face significant challenges when handling complex prompts—particularly those involving multiple subjects with distinct attributes. Inspired by the human drawing process, which first outlines the composition and then incrementally adds details, we propose Detail++, a training-free framework that introduces a novel Progressive Detail Injection (PDI) strategy to address this limitation. Specifically, we decompose a complex prompt into a sequence of simplified sub-prompts, guiding the generation process in stages. This staged generation leverages the inherent layout-controlling capacity of self-attention to first ensure global composition, followed by precise refinement. To achieve accurate binding between attributes and corresponding subjects, we exploit cross-attention mechanisms and further introduce a Centroid Alignment Loss at test time to reduce binding noise and enhance attribute consistency. Extensive experiments on T2I-CompBench and a newly constructed style composition benchmark demonstrate that Detail++ significantly outperforms existing methods, particularly in scenarios involving multiple objects and complex stylistic conditions. Published: 2026-06-04T19:58:29+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lifeng Chen; Jiner Wang; Zihao Pan; Beier Zhu; Xiaofeng Yang; Chi Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3698370

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have led to impressive visual results. However, these models still face significant challenges when handling complex prompts—particularly those involving multiple subjects with distinct attributes. Inspired by the human drawing process, which first outlines the composition and then incrementally adds details, we propose Detail++, a training-free framework that introduces a novel Progressive Detail Injection (PDI) strategy to address this limitation. Specifically, we decompose a complex prompt into a sequence of simplified sub-prompts, guiding the generation process in stages. This staged generation leverages the inherent layout-controlling capacity of self-attention to first ensure global composition, followed by precise refinement. To achieve accurate binding between attributes and corresponding subjects, we exploit cross-attention mechanisms and further introduce a Centroid Alignment Loss at test time to reduce binding noise and enhance attribute consistency. Extensive experiments on T2I-CompBench and a newly constructed style composition benchmark demonstrate that Detail++ significantly outperforms existing methods, particularly in scenarios involving multiple objects and complex stylistic conditions.

item.title: Visual Camera Localization by Globally Consistent Descriptor Learning and Combined Bundle Adjustment
item.dc:creator: Junyi Wang
Yuze Wang
Chen Wang
Yue Qi
item.description: In this paper, we introduce a brand-new localization pipeline designed to comprehensively leverage both hand-crafted and learned features, operating at two distinct levels (point-level and object-level) while simultaneously recovering scene scale from the RGB input. The pipeline integrates a learned globally consistent descriptor matching process for initial camera pose estimation, followed by a pose optimization phase that synergistically combines various features. To generate learned descriptors, we propose a siamese Globally Consistent Feature Descriptor Network (GCFDNet), which accepts a pair of images, Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data, and pose sequences as inputs, producing both the image descriptors and the relative camera pose as outputs. The strengths of GCFDNet manifest in two key aspects. First, by incorporating a spatial-to-temporal feature fusion module, GCFDNet enhances relative pose regression, meanwhile enabling accurate scene scale estimation. Second, we devise a loss function that balances descriptor similarity and distance, thereby improving the quality of descriptor learning. Using the initial camera poses derived from GCFDNet, we establish data associations across multiple frames and subsequently propose a combined Bundle Adjustment (BA) optimization framework that integrates hand-crafted features, learned descriptors, and semantic objects. To evaluate the localization performance, we conduct experiments across diverse datasets, including EuRoC, ScanNet, 7 Scenes, TUM RGB-D, and Bonn. The results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both static and dynamic scenes, outperforming existing methods. Additionally, we present ablation studies on GCFDNet and the combined BA process to further substantiate the efficacy of our approach. Published: 2026-06-04T19:58:29+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Junyi Wang; Yuze Wang; Chen Wang; Yue Qi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3698365

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

In this paper, we introduce a brand-new localization pipeline designed to comprehensively leverage both hand-crafted and learned features, operating at two distinct levels (point-level and object-level) while simultaneously recovering scene scale from the RGB input. The pipeline integrates a learned globally consistent descriptor matching process for initial camera pose estimation, followed by a pose optimization phase that synergistically combines various features. To generate learned descriptors, we propose a siamese Globally Consistent Feature Descriptor Network (GCFDNet), which accepts a pair of images, Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data, and pose sequences as inputs, producing both the image descriptors and the relative camera pose as outputs. The strengths of GCFDNet manifest in two key aspects. First, by incorporating a spatial-to-temporal feature fusion module, GCFDNet enhances relative pose regression, meanwhile enabling accurate scene scale estimation. Second, we devise a loss function that balances descriptor similarity and distance, thereby improving the quality of descriptor learning. Using the initial camera poses derived from GCFDNet, we establish data associations across multiple frames and subsequently propose a combined Bundle Adjustment (BA) optimization framework that integrates hand-crafted features, learned descriptors, and semantic objects. To evaluate the localization performance, we conduct experiments across diverse datasets, including EuRoC, ScanNet, 7 Scenes, TUM RGB-D, and Bonn. The results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both static and dynamic scenes, outperforming existing methods. Additionally, we present ablation studies on GCFDNet and the combined BA process to further substantiate the efficacy of our approach.

item.title: LandslideSegFormer: A non-local context-aware dual-branch network for accurate small-scale landslide segmentation from remote sensing imagery
item.dc:creator: Xuemei Lee
Lin Bai
Dinghong Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Small-scale landslide segmentation remains a classic yet challenging task in remote sensing image analysis. Current transformer-based landslide segmentation models suffer from two main limitations: difficulty in detecting small targets and the loss of boundary details. To address these issues, we propose LandslideSegFormer, a semantic segmentation network inspired by the SegFormer framework but redesigned in both encoder and decoder modules. LandslideSegFormer jointly leverages transformers and convolutional structures to capture both global and local information, thereby enhancing the representation of small-scale landslide features. More importantly, we introduce a non-local block as a global feature interaction mechanism, which performs similarity-driven aggregation across feature maps. By computing pairwise similarities in the feature space, the mechanism adaptively controls information exchange such that semantically similar locations directly “share” features. With a single-layer operation, it establishes long-range dependencies whose strength is determined by feature content rather than spatial distance, enabling the modeling of complex relationships between landslides and environmental factors. Furthermore, we design a gated hybrid-attention decoder tailored for LandslideSegFormer’s encoder. Through gated propagation, the decoder selectively controls multi-scale feature transmission, preserves high-resolution details, suppresses background noise, and accurately localizes small-scale landslide regions. Extensive experiments on the large-scale CAS Landslide dataset, which includes both satellite and UAV imagery, systematically evaluate LandslideSegFormer from the perspectives of small-object segmentation and complex boundary delineation. LandslideSegFormer, with a lightweight B0-based design, outperforms SegFormer-B0 and B2, improving landslide IoU by 5.34 percentage points on the complete test set and 8.16 percentage points on small-scale landslides (with boundary IoU improved by 9.08 percentage points), demonstrating superior capability in detecting small-scale landslides and complex boundaries. Published: 2026-06-04T11:32:46+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuemei Lee; Lin Bai; Dinghong Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.047

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Small-scale landslide segmentation remains a classic yet challenging task in remote sensing image analysis. Current transformer-based landslide segmentation models suffer from two main limitations: difficulty in detecting small targets and the loss of boundary details. To address these issues, we propose LandslideSegFormer, a semantic segmentation network inspired by the SegFormer framework but redesigned in both encoder and decoder modules. LandslideSegFormer jointly leverages transformers and convolutional structures to capture both global and local information, thereby enhancing the representation of small-scale landslide features. More importantly, we introduce a non-local block as a global feature interaction mechanism, which performs similarity-driven aggregation across feature maps. By computing pairwise similarities in the feature space, the mechanism adaptively controls information exchange such that semantically similar locations directly “share” features. With a single-layer operation, it establishes long-range dependencies whose strength is determined by feature content rather than spatial distance, enabling the modeling of complex relationships between landslides and environmental factors. Furthermore, we design a gated hybrid-attention decoder tailored for LandslideSegFormer’s encoder. Through gated propagation, the decoder selectively controls multi-scale feature transmission, preserves high-resolution details, suppresses background noise, and accurately localizes small-scale landslide regions. Extensive experiments on the large-scale CAS Landslide dataset, which includes both satellite and UAV imagery, systematically evaluate LandslideSegFormer from the perspectives of small-object segmentation and complex boundary delineation. LandslideSegFormer, with a lightweight B0-based design, outperforms SegFormer-B0 and B2, improving landslide IoU by 5.34 percentage points on the complete test set and 8.16 percentage points on small-scale landslides (with boundary IoU improved by 9.08 percentage points), demonstrating superior capability in detecting small-scale landslides and complex boundaries.

item.title: Mamba-SCR: a frequency-domain enhanced state space model for cloud removal with SAR and optical data fusion
item.dc:creator: Chengyao Zhang
Fengyan Wang
Xuqing Zhang
Ruifei Zhu
Mingchang Wang
Xiang Wu
Qing Ding
Qing Wang
item.description: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery provides valuable complementary information for optical remote sensing image cloud removal because of its all-weather observation capability. However, the substantial modality discrepancy between SAR and optical imagery, coupled with SAR-specific speckle noise, makes reliable multimodal fusion and accurate reconstruction of cloud-contaminated regions highly challenging. To address these issues, this study proposes Mamba-SCR, a SAR-assisted cloud removal framework that couples frequency-domain enhancement with state space modeling. Specifically, a wavelet-based gated enhancement module is introduced to suppress speckle-related interference while retaining structurally informative details, and an optical-SAR feature fusion strategy is designed to adaptively exploit complementary multimodal cues. Moreover, a spatial-frequency state space modeling scheme is developed to jointly capture global context and long-range dependencies for reconstructing large cloud-covered areas. Experiments on the public SEN12MS-CR dataset and the self-constructed CTR-CR dataset demonstrate that Mamba-SCR achieves consistent improvements under diverse cloud-cover levels and land-cover types. Compared with the strongest baseline, Mamba-SCR improves PSNR by 1.42 dB and 1.11 dB on SEN12MS-CR and CTR-CR, respectively, confirming its effectiveness for SAR-assisted cloud removal. Published: 2026-06-04T06:28:44+00:00 Venue: GIScience & Remote Sensing Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengyao Zhang; Fengyan Wang; Xuqing Zhang; Ruifei Zhu; Mingchang Wang; Xiang Wu; Qing Ding; Qing Wang

Journal: GIScience & Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1080/15481603.2026.2677334

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery provides valuable complementary information for optical remote sensing image cloud removal because of its all-weather observation capability. However, the substantial modality discrepancy between SAR and optical imagery, coupled with SAR-specific speckle noise, makes reliable multimodal fusion and accurate reconstruction of cloud-contaminated regions highly challenging. To address these issues, this study proposes Mamba-SCR, a SAR-assisted cloud removal framework that couples frequency-domain enhancement with state space modeling. Specifically, a wavelet-based gated enhancement module is introduced to suppress speckle-related interference while retaining structurally informative details, and an optical-SAR feature fusion strategy is designed to adaptively exploit complementary multimodal cues. Moreover, a spatial-frequency state space modeling scheme is developed to jointly capture global context and long-range dependencies for reconstructing large cloud-covered areas. Experiments on the public SEN12MS-CR dataset and the self-constructed CTR-CR dataset demonstrate that Mamba-SCR achieves consistent improvements under diverse cloud-cover levels and land-cover types. Compared with the strongest baseline, Mamba-SCR improves PSNR by 1.42 dB and 1.11 dB on SEN12MS-CR and CTR-CR, respectively, confirming its effectiveness for SAR-assisted cloud removal.

item.title: TRAN: Texture Response Adjustment Network for Lightweight Single Image Super-Resolution
item.dc:creator: Ping Zheng
Rongfeng Zhou
He Jiang
Wen Cui
Liangliang Chen
Qiqi Kou
Deqiang Cheng
Guangtao Zhai
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) aims to reconstruct a high-resolution (HR) image from a given low-resolution (LR) input. Reconstruction difficulty varies across textures of different complexity. However, existing methods apply a single, unified feature modeling and reconstruction pipeline for all textures. This often exacerbates the reconstruction imbalance across textures of varying complexity. To address this issue, this study proposes Texture Response Adjustment Network for SISR (TRAN). This method achieves more balanced fine-grained reconstruction and improves overall reconstruction quality by adaptively adjusting texture response. TRAN comprises two main components. The first mitigates local texture response bias within an image by decoupling textures of different complexity in the frequency domain for independent modeling and response adjustment. The second alleviates global texture response bias across different images by dynamically perceiving overall image complexity and adjusting the network structure accordingly. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed TRAN balances fine-grained reconstruction across textures of different complexity and improve overall reconstruction performance. Notably, on the ×4 SISR task of the Manga109 dataset, TRAN outperforms MambaIRv2-L by 0.31 dB in PSNR while reducing FLOPs by 38.1G. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ping Zheng; Rongfeng Zhou; He Jiang; Wen Cui; Liangliang Chen; Qiqi Kou; Deqiang Cheng; Guangtao Zhai

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700195

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) aims to reconstruct a high-resolution (HR) image from a given low-resolution (LR) input. Reconstruction difficulty varies across textures of different complexity. However, existing methods apply a single, unified feature modeling and reconstruction pipeline for all textures. This often exacerbates the reconstruction imbalance across textures of varying complexity. To address this issue, this study proposes Texture Response Adjustment Network for SISR (TRAN). This method achieves more balanced fine-grained reconstruction and improves overall reconstruction quality by adaptively adjusting texture response. TRAN comprises two main components. The first mitigates local texture response bias within an image by decoupling textures of different complexity in the frequency domain for independent modeling and response adjustment. The second alleviates global texture response bias across different images by dynamically perceiving overall image complexity and adjusting the network structure accordingly. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed TRAN balances fine-grained reconstruction across textures of different complexity and improve overall reconstruction performance. Notably, on the ×4 SISR task of the Manga109 dataset, TRAN outperforms MambaIRv2-L by 0.31 dB in PSNR while reducing FLOPs by 38.1G.

item.title: DifferSeg: Towards Diverse Multimodal Binary Segmentation via Differential Perception and Frequency Guidance
item.dc:creator: Qiangqiang Zhou
Jiawei Xu
Yong Chen
Dandan Zhu
Yugen Yi
Xiaoqi Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: In many binary segmentation tasks, most multimodal methods rely on fixed feature concatenation for cross-modal interaction and straightforward decoder designs dominated by low-frequency semantics. However, they ignore two key challenges: one is the lack of an adaptive mechanism to handle modality discrepancies and complementarity, and the other is the absence of an efficient decoding strategy to balance both high-and low-frequency representations. In this work, we propose a simple yet general multimodal binary segmentation framework, termed DifferSeg, to address both problems simultaneously. With the help of the differential perception fusion (DPF) module, DifferSeg employs learnable differential operators to adaptively align multimodal features and enhance their complementarity through residual fusion, effectively mitigating modality mismatch and fusion redundancy. In addition, we design a frequency-guided decoder (FGD) that builds cross-frequency interactions and multi-path upsampling to maintain consistency between detailed high-frequency structures and semantic low-frequency representations, ensuring fine-grained boundary recovery and noise suppression. Benefiting from these designs, DifferSeg can be easily generalized to diverse binary segmentation tasks, including both natural and medical modalities. Without bells and whistles, it consistently surpasses 67 state-of-the-art methods across 29 public datasets involving 18 downstream tasks, demonstrating superior generalization and segmentation accuracy. Code and pretrained models will be available at the link. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.790 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700609

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

In many binary segmentation tasks, most multimodal methods rely on fixed feature concatenation for cross-modal interaction and straightforward decoder designs dominated by low-frequency semantics. However, they ignore two key challenges: one is the lack of an adaptive mechanism to handle modality discrepancies and complementarity, and the other is the absence of an efficient decoding strategy to balance both high-and low-frequency representations. In this work, we propose a simple yet general multimodal binary segmentation framework, termed DifferSeg, to address both problems simultaneously. With the help of the differential perception fusion (DPF) module, DifferSeg employs learnable differential operators to adaptively align multimodal features and enhance their complementarity through residual fusion, effectively mitigating modality mismatch and fusion redundancy. In addition, we design a frequency-guided decoder (FGD) that builds cross-frequency interactions and multi-path upsampling to maintain consistency between detailed high-frequency structures and semantic low-frequency representations, ensuring fine-grained boundary recovery and noise suppression. Benefiting from these designs, DifferSeg can be easily generalized to diverse binary segmentation tasks, including both natural and medical modalities. Without bells and whistles, it consistently surpasses 67 state-of-the-art methods across 29 public datasets involving 18 downstream tasks, demonstrating superior generalization and segmentation accuracy. Code and pretrained models will be available at the link.

item.title: Few-Shot Object Detection Based on Lie Group Manifold Feature Reweighting and Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis for Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Xiliang Chen
Longwei Li
Yufeng Chen
Wenhuan Wu
Zhaowen Deng
Lei Liu
Guoyang Liu
Zhenyu Wang
Bo Feng
Kaixuan Wang
item.description: Few-shot object detection (FSOD) techniques have achieved significant progress in the field of natural images, yet their performance remains limited in remote sensing image object detection. This is primarily due to the complex scenes in remote sensing images,which exhibit significant intra-class variability and inter-class similarity, thereby hindering the improvement of detection accuracy in FSOD methods under few-shot conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposes a FSOD framework for remote sensing images that integrates Lie group manifold feature reweighting (LGMFR) and Lie group kernel Fisher discriminant analysis (LGMFR-LGKFDA). Firstly, convolutional features are mapped onto symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices on the Lie group manifold through covariance pooling, establishing the manifold geometric structure of the feature space. The Lie group intrinsic mean (LGIM) algorithm is then employed to construct more representative class prototypes in the manifold space, enhancing intra-class compactness. Secondly, the prototypes on the Lie group manifold are transformed into feature vectors, these vectors are used to reweight the features of the query set, effectively suppressing the background noise and highlighting critical target features. Finally, the Lie group kernel Fisher discriminant analysis (LGKFDA) optimization module is applied to achieve tighter clustering of samples from the same class and greater separation between different classes in the geometric space, thereby improving the model’s classification performance and generalization ability. Experiments on NWPU VHR-10 and DIOR datasets show that the proposed method has the best performance compared with several state-of-the-art methods. Published: 2026-06-04T15:20:41+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.790 (must_read)

Authors: Xiliang Chen; Longwei Li; Yufeng Chen; Wenhuan Wu; Zhaowen Deng; Lei Liu; Guoyang Liu; Zhenyu Wang; Bo Feng; Kaixuan Wang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133088

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Few-shot object detection (FSOD) techniques have achieved significant progress in the field of natural images, yet their performance remains limited in remote sensing image object detection. This is primarily due to the complex scenes in remote sensing images,which exhibit significant intra-class variability and inter-class similarity, thereby hindering the improvement of detection accuracy in FSOD methods under few-shot conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposes a FSOD framework for remote sensing images that integrates Lie group manifold feature reweighting (LGMFR) and Lie group kernel Fisher discriminant analysis (LGMFR-LGKFDA). Firstly, convolutional features are mapped onto symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices on the Lie group manifold through covariance pooling, establishing the manifold geometric structure of the feature space. The Lie group intrinsic mean (LGIM) algorithm is then employed to construct more representative class prototypes in the manifold space, enhancing intra-class compactness. Secondly, the prototypes on the Lie group manifold are transformed into feature vectors, these vectors are used to reweight the features of the query set, effectively suppressing the background noise and highlighting critical target features. Finally, the Lie group kernel Fisher discriminant analysis (LGKFDA) optimization module is applied to achieve tighter clustering of samples from the same class and greater separation between different classes in the geometric space, thereby improving the model’s classification performance and generalization ability. Experiments on NWPU VHR-10 and DIOR datasets show that the proposed method has the best performance compared with several state-of-the-art methods.

item.title: Flow Matching Posterior Sampling: A Training-free Conditional Generation for Flow Matching
item.dc:creator: Kaiyu Song
Hanjiang Lai
Yan Pan
Kun Yue
Jian Yin
item.description: Training-free conditional generation based on flow matching aims to leverage pre-trained unconditional flow matching models to perform conditional generation without retraining. Recently, a successful training-free conditional generation approach incorporates conditions via posterior sampling, which relies on the availability of a score function in the unconditional diffusion model. However, flow matching models lack an explicit score function, rendering this strategy inapplicable. Approximate posterior sampling for flow matching has been explored, but it is limited to linear inverse problems. In this paper, we propose Flow Matching-based Posterior Sampling (FMPS) to broaden its scope of application. We introduce a correction term by steering the velocity field. This correction term can be reformulated to incorporate a surrogate score function, thereby bridging the gap between flow matching models and score-based posterior sampling. Hence, FMPS enables posterior sampling to be adjusted within the flow-matching framework. Furthermore, we propose two practical implementations of the correction mechanism: one to improve generation quality and the other to enhance computational efficiency. Experimental results on diverse conditional generation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves superior generation quality compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches, validating the effectiveness and generality of FMPS. Published: 2026-06-04T19:58:29+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.789 (must_read)

Authors: Kaiyu Song; Hanjiang Lai; Yan Pan; Kun Yue; Jian Yin

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3698367

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Training-free conditional generation based on flow matching aims to leverage pre-trained unconditional flow matching models to perform conditional generation without retraining. Recently, a successful training-free conditional generation approach incorporates conditions via posterior sampling, which relies on the availability of a score function in the unconditional diffusion model. However, flow matching models lack an explicit score function, rendering this strategy inapplicable. Approximate posterior sampling for flow matching has been explored, but it is limited to linear inverse problems. In this paper, we propose Flow Matching-based Posterior Sampling (FMPS) to broaden its scope of application. We introduce a correction term by steering the velocity field. This correction term can be reformulated to incorporate a surrogate score function, thereby bridging the gap between flow matching models and score-based posterior sampling. Hence, FMPS enables posterior sampling to be adjusted within the flow-matching framework. Furthermore, we propose two practical implementations of the correction mechanism: one to improve generation quality and the other to enhance computational efficiency. Experimental results on diverse conditional generation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves superior generation quality compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches, validating the effectiveness and generality of FMPS.

item.title: Efficient Image Dehazing via Prior-Guided Hierarchical Harmonization Network
item.dc:creator: Chen Wu
Xiongfei Su
Yuning Cui
Mingyue Cui
Wenqi Ren
Jianhou Gan
Xin Yuan
Yulun Zhang
Jingyuan Xia
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: This paper proposes an efficient image dehazing method based on networked physical priors and coordinated fusion, aiming to resolve the long-standing trade-off between performance and computational complexity in deep learning-based dehazing. Existing approaches typically treat physical priors as fixed constraints or introduce them only in shallow layers, failing to achieve hierarchical integration with deep features, which forces networks to stack more modules for better performance at the cost of heavy computation. To address this, our work introduces two key innovations. First, we design three learnable prior operators that transform the Dark Channel Prior, Bright Channel Prior, and Histogram Equalization prior into adaptive modules embedded within the network, enabling dynamic modeling of haze distribution, structural details, and feature statistics. Second, we construct an asymmetric UNet-based fusion architecture, which employs gated attention and a dual-bottleneck design to achieve organic multi-level integration of prior information and deep features throughout the encoding–decoding pipeline. Since the physical priors are structurally encoded into the network, the model does not require a large number of parameters to implicitly learn haze-related patterns. Combined with redundancy suppression through the asymmetric design, this naturally yields a lightweight yet high-performance dehazing network. Extensive experiments on 11 benchmark datasets, including SOTS and Dense-Haze, demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art dehazing performance while maintaining only 1.69M parameters and 17G FLOPs, significantly surpassing existing methods. This provides an effective solution for high-quality image dehazing under resource-constrained scenarios. Published: 2026-06-04T19:57:53+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chen Wu; Xiongfei Su; Yuning Cui; Mingyue Cui; Wenqi Ren; Jianhou Gan; Xin Yuan; Yulun Zhang; Jingyuan Xia

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700528

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

This paper proposes an efficient image dehazing method based on networked physical priors and coordinated fusion, aiming to resolve the long-standing trade-off between performance and computational complexity in deep learning-based dehazing. Existing approaches typically treat physical priors as fixed constraints or introduce them only in shallow layers, failing to achieve hierarchical integration with deep features, which forces networks to stack more modules for better performance at the cost of heavy computation. To address this, our work introduces two key innovations. First, we design three learnable prior operators that transform the Dark Channel Prior, Bright Channel Prior, and Histogram Equalization prior into adaptive modules embedded within the network, enabling dynamic modeling of haze distribution, structural details, and feature statistics. Second, we construct an asymmetric UNet-based fusion architecture, which employs gated attention and a dual-bottleneck design to achieve organic multi-level integration of prior information and deep features throughout the encoding–decoding pipeline. Since the physical priors are structurally encoded into the network, the model does not require a large number of parameters to implicitly learn haze-related patterns. Combined with redundancy suppression through the asymmetric design, this naturally yields a lightweight yet high-performance dehazing network. Extensive experiments on 11 benchmark datasets, including SOTS and Dense-Haze, demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art dehazing performance while maintaining only 1.69M parameters and 17G FLOPs, significantly surpassing existing methods. This provides an effective solution for high-quality image dehazing under resource-constrained scenarios.

item.title: Breaking the Vicious Cycle: Coherent 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse and Motion-Blurred Views
item.dc:creator: Chaoran Feng
Zhankuo Xu
Yingtao Li
Jianbin Zhao
Jiashu Yang
Wangbo Yu
Li Yuan
Yonghong Tian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a state-of-the-art method for novel view synthesis. However, its performance heavily relies on dense, high-quality input imagery, an assumption that is often violated in real-world applications, where data is typically sparse and motion-blurred. These two issues create a vicious cycle: sparse views ignore the multi-view constraints necessary to resolve motion blur, while motion blur erases high-frequency details crucial for aligning the limited views. Thus, reconstruction often fails catastrophically, with fragmented views and a low-frequency bias. To break this cycle, we introduce CoherentGS, a novel framework for high-fidelity 3D reconstruction from sparse and blurry images. Our key insight is to address these compound degradations using a dual-prior strategy. Specifically, we combine two pre-trained generative models: a specialized deblurring network for restoring sharp details and providing photometric guidance, and a diffusion model that offers geometric priors to fill in unobserved regions of the scene. This dual-prior strategy is supported by several key techniques, including a consistency-guided camera exploration module that adaptively guides the generative process, and a depth regularization loss that ensures geometric plausibility. We evaluate CoherentGS through both quantitative and qualitative experiments on synthetic and real-world scenes, using as few as 3, 6, and 9 input views. Our results demonstrate that CoherentGS significantly outperforms existing methods, setting a new state-of-the-art for this challenging task. The code and video demos are available at https://potatobigroom.github.io/CoherentGS/. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.788 (must_read)

Authors: Chaoran Feng; Zhankuo Xu; Yingtao Li; Jianbin Zhao; Jiashu Yang; Wangbo Yu; Li Yuan; Yonghong Tian

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700148

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a state-of-the-art method for novel view synthesis. However, its performance heavily relies on dense, high-quality input imagery, an assumption that is often violated in real-world applications, where data is typically sparse and motion-blurred. These two issues create a vicious cycle: sparse views ignore the multi-view constraints necessary to resolve motion blur, while motion blur erases high-frequency details crucial for aligning the limited views. Thus, reconstruction often fails catastrophically, with fragmented views and a low-frequency bias. To break this cycle, we introduce CoherentGS, a novel framework for high-fidelity 3D reconstruction from sparse and blurry images. Our key insight is to address these compound degradations using a dual-prior strategy. Specifically, we combine two pre-trained generative models: a specialized deblurring network for restoring sharp details and providing photometric guidance, and a diffusion model that offers geometric priors to fill in unobserved regions of the scene. This dual-prior strategy is supported by several key techniques, including a consistency-guided camera exploration module that adaptively guides the generative process, and a depth regularization loss that ensures geometric plausibility. We evaluate CoherentGS through both quantitative and qualitative experiments on synthetic and real-world scenes, using as few as 3, 6, and 9 input views. Our results demonstrate that CoherentGS significantly outperforms existing methods, setting a new state-of-the-art for this challenging task. The code and video demos are available at https://potatobigroom.github.io/CoherentGS/.

item.title: HCDi-Fusion: Hybrid-Conditioned Diffusion Model for Cross-Resolution Multimodal Image Fusion
item.dc:creator: Duzhong Feng
Chenxi Qiu
Tao Yue
Xuemei Hu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: In multimodal image fusion tasks, introducing diffusion models faces a critical challenge due to the lack of ground truth (GT) for step-wise supervision during the diffusion process. Existing methods typically unfold diffusion models in two ways: either supervising only the final fused result while neglecting intermediate diffusion steps, or adopting mask-based fusion strategies that rely on pre-generated masks to supervise intermediate predicted masks. However, the latter heavily depends on the quality of the pre-generated masks. Moreover, in real-world scenarios, the spatial resolutions of different modalities often exhibit significant discrepancies, which further complicates the generation of reliable pre-generated masks. To address these challenges, we propose HCDi-Fusion for cross-resolution multimodal image fusion. Specifically, the Hybrid-Conditioned Diffusion (HCD) model performs guided denoising by jointly leveraging self-conditioning and external conditioning, which considers inter-step relationships and different resolution discrepancies. Furthermore, the proposed Self-conditioned Step-Wise Fusion (SSWF) model enables progressive fusion in the latent feature space across diffusion steps. To accommodate the inherently unfolded nature of diffusion models in fusion tasks, we further design a Step-Wise Supervision Mechanism to supervise intermediate diffusion steps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple fusion tasks, diverse resolution discrepancies, multiple datasets, and a wide range of evaluation metrics. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Duzhong Feng; Chenxi Qiu; Tao Yue; Xuemei Hu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700715

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

In multimodal image fusion tasks, introducing diffusion models faces a critical challenge due to the lack of ground truth (GT) for step-wise supervision during the diffusion process. Existing methods typically unfold diffusion models in two ways: either supervising only the final fused result while neglecting intermediate diffusion steps, or adopting mask-based fusion strategies that rely on pre-generated masks to supervise intermediate predicted masks. However, the latter heavily depends on the quality of the pre-generated masks. Moreover, in real-world scenarios, the spatial resolutions of different modalities often exhibit significant discrepancies, which further complicates the generation of reliable pre-generated masks. To address these challenges, we propose HCDi-Fusion for cross-resolution multimodal image fusion. Specifically, the Hybrid-Conditioned Diffusion (HCD) model performs guided denoising by jointly leveraging self-conditioning and external conditioning, which considers inter-step relationships and different resolution discrepancies. Furthermore, the proposed Self-conditioned Step-Wise Fusion (SSWF) model enables progressive fusion in the latent feature space across diffusion steps. To accommodate the inherently unfolded nature of diffusion models in fusion tasks, we further design a Step-Wise Supervision Mechanism to supervise intermediate diffusion steps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple fusion tasks, diverse resolution discrepancies, multiple datasets, and a wide range of evaluation metrics.

item.title: Strong Saddle-Shaped Geometry of Depth Maps Can Boost Multi-View Stereo
item.dc:creator: Zekun Gu
Tiao Tan
Zongqing Lu
Jing-Hao Xue
Qingmin Liao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Multi-view stereo (MVS) aims to accurately estimate depth maps for high-quality 3D reconstruction. Recently, saddle-shaped depth maps have been proved to improve the quality of 3D reconstruction. However, existing methods often suffer from weak saddle-shaped geometry in the estimated depth maps and high redundancy in network design, leading to suboptimal reconstruction performance and low computational efficiency. In this paper, we propose SDMVSNet, a new model to show that stronger saddle-shaped depth map can achieve better MVS performance while maintaining high model efficiency. Specifically, we introduce a novel multi-granularity loss function to enforce strong and comprehensive saddle-shaped geometry of the depth map. Moreover, we propose a spatial folding regularization, which leverages the spectral bias of neural networks to produce smooth submaps for better reconstruction of saddle-shaped depth map. In addition, we propose a saddle-aware feature transformer to effectively capture long-range saddle-shaped geometric information via a lightweight attention mechanism. Extensive experiments on the DTU, BlendedMVS, Tanks and Temples and ETH3D datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art MVS performance with high efficiency. Published: 2026-06-05T19:41:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zekun Gu; Tiao Tan; Zongqing Lu; Jing-Hao Xue; Qingmin Liao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3700782

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Multi-view stereo (MVS) aims to accurately estimate depth maps for high-quality 3D reconstruction. Recently, saddle-shaped depth maps have been proved to improve the quality of 3D reconstruction. However, existing methods often suffer from weak saddle-shaped geometry in the estimated depth maps and high redundancy in network design, leading to suboptimal reconstruction performance and low computational efficiency. In this paper, we propose SDMVSNet, a new model to show that stronger saddle-shaped depth map can achieve better MVS performance while maintaining high model efficiency. Specifically, we introduce a novel multi-granularity loss function to enforce strong and comprehensive saddle-shaped geometry of the depth map. Moreover, we propose a spatial folding regularization, which leverages the spectral bias of neural networks to produce smooth submaps for better reconstruction of saddle-shaped depth map. In addition, we propose a saddle-aware feature transformer to effectively capture long-range saddle-shaped geometric information via a lightweight attention mechanism. Extensive experiments on the DTU, BlendedMVS, Tanks and Temples and ETH3D datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art MVS performance with high efficiency.

item.title: FreqChange: Frequency-aware spatiotemporal learning with prior guidance for semantic change detection in remote sensing images
item.dc:creator: Haiyan Huang
Zhenfeng Shao
Duowang Zhu
Xiao Huang
Ruiqian Zhang
Qimin Cheng
Jindou Zhang
Yueming Duan
Deren Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Semantic change detection plays a critical role in monitoring Earth’s surface dynamics by identifying the locations and semantic categories of changes from bi-temporal remote sensing images. Existing methods primarily employ a shared-weight image encoder coupled with a change extractor to detect changes in the spatial domain, while lacking sufficient temporal modeling and effective exploitation of semantic priors. As a result, they struggle to accurately localize changed regions and maintain intra-class semantic consistency. To address these limitations, we propose FreqChange, a frequency-aware spatiotemporal learning framework with semantic prior guidance. Specifically, a frequency change-aware module (FCAM) is integrated into the spatiotemporal learning framework to accurately perceive and localize changed regions by comprehensively analyzing both low- and high-frequency components between bi-temporal features. Additionally, to enhance the intra-class semantic consistency of bi-temporal images, we propose a semantic consistency enhancement module (SCEM), which leverages semantic priors derived from large-scale pre-trained vision foundation models, combined with semantic consistency upsampling, to effectively facilitate spatiotemporal learning. Through their collaborative integration, these modules collectively enable the precise detection of land cover changes from bi-temporal images while preserving intra-class semantic consistency. Extensive experiments on four well-recognized datasets ( i.e. , HRSCD, SECOND, HiUCD-mini, and Landsat-SCD) demonstrate that FreqChange consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations. By uniting frequency-domain analysis and semantic priors, FreqChange establishes a new paradigm for accurate and reliable semantic change detection in remote sensing. Published: 2026-06-05T17:20:29+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.784 (must_read)

Authors: Haiyan Huang; Zhenfeng Shao; Duowang Zhu; Xiao Huang; Ruiqian Zhang; Qimin Cheng; Jindou Zhang; Yueming Duan; Deren Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.042

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

Semantic change detection plays a critical role in monitoring Earth’s surface dynamics by identifying the locations and semantic categories of changes from bi-temporal remote sensing images. Existing methods primarily employ a shared-weight image encoder coupled with a change extractor to detect changes in the spatial domain, while lacking sufficient temporal modeling and effective exploitation of semantic priors. As a result, they struggle to accurately localize changed regions and maintain intra-class semantic consistency. To address these limitations, we propose FreqChange, a frequency-aware spatiotemporal learning framework with semantic prior guidance. Specifically, a frequency change-aware module (FCAM) is integrated into the spatiotemporal learning framework to accurately perceive and localize changed regions by comprehensively analyzing both low- and high-frequency components between bi-temporal features. Additionally, to enhance the intra-class semantic consistency of bi-temporal images, we propose a semantic consistency enhancement module (SCEM), which leverages semantic priors derived from large-scale pre-trained vision foundation models, combined with semantic consistency upsampling, to effectively facilitate spatiotemporal learning. Through their collaborative integration, these modules collectively enable the precise detection of land cover changes from bi-temporal images while preserving intra-class semantic consistency. Extensive experiments on four well-recognized datasets ( i.e. , HRSCD, SECOND, HiUCD-mini, and Landsat-SCD) demonstrate that FreqChange consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations. By uniting frequency-domain analysis and semantic priors, FreqChange establishes a new paradigm for accurate and reliable semantic change detection in remote sensing.

item.title: Marine Saliency Segmenter: Object-Focused Conditional Diffusion with Region-Level Semantic Knowledge Distillation
item.dc:creator: Laibin Chang
Yunke Wang
Jiaxing Huang
Longxiang Deng
Bo Du
Chang Xu
item.description: Marine Saliency Segmentation (MSS) plays a pivotal role in a wide range of vision-based marine exploration tasks. However, existing techniques often face the dilemma of imprecise boundaries due to the interference-rich nature of underwater environments, where suspended particles, low contrast, and color distortion hinder accurate segmentation. Although diffusion models have shown impressive performance in visual tasks, their potential to incorporate contextual semantics for enhancing feature learning of region-level salient objects remains under-explored, thereby hindering segmentation outcomes. Building on this insight, we propose DiffMSS, a novel marine saliency segmenter based on the diffusion model, which utilizes semantic knowledge distillation to guide the detection of marine salient objects. Specifically, we design the Word-level Semantic Saliency Extraction module that identifies salient terms at the word level from the captions by computing region-word similarity. These high-level semantic features are distilled into the Conditional Feature Learning Network to generate accurate and semantically informed diffusion conditions. The Object-Focused Conditional Diffusion module then leverages these conditions to iteratively generate fine-grained segmentation masks of marine instances, while a Consensus Deterministic Sampling scheme is further employed to suppress overconfident mis-segmentations and enhance structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of DiffMSS over state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Our code and pre-trained models will be released on GitHub. Published: 2026-06-04T19:58:29+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Laibin Chang; Yunke Wang; Jiaxing Huang; Longxiang Deng; Bo Du; Chang Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3698359

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Marine Saliency Segmentation (MSS) plays a pivotal role in a wide range of vision-based marine exploration tasks. However, existing techniques often face the dilemma of imprecise boundaries due to the interference-rich nature of underwater environments, where suspended particles, low contrast, and color distortion hinder accurate segmentation. Although diffusion models have shown impressive performance in visual tasks, their potential to incorporate contextual semantics for enhancing feature learning of region-level salient objects remains under-explored, thereby hindering segmentation outcomes. Building on this insight, we propose DiffMSS, a novel marine saliency segmenter based on the diffusion model, which utilizes semantic knowledge distillation to guide the detection of marine salient objects. Specifically, we design the Word-level Semantic Saliency Extraction module that identifies salient terms at the word level from the captions by computing region-word similarity. These high-level semantic features are distilled into the Conditional Feature Learning Network to generate accurate and semantically informed diffusion conditions. The Object-Focused Conditional Diffusion module then leverages these conditions to iteratively generate fine-grained segmentation masks of marine instances, while a Consensus Deterministic Sampling scheme is further employed to suppress overconfident mis-segmentations and enhance structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of DiffMSS over state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Our code and pre-trained models will be released on GitHub.

item.title: Siamese Mamba with dynamic relational matcher for hyperspectral object tracking
item.dc:creator: Minghao Zhu
Chongchong Wang
Heng Wang
Yuebo Meng
Yaohai Lin
Junyi Li
Zongfang Ma
item.description: Hyperspectral object tracking (HOT) faces significant challenges due to scarce data and the domain shift that arises when transferring RGB pre-trained models. To address these issues, we propose a novel two-stage framework SiamMDR, which introduces a task-specific pre-training strategy and a dynamic relational matching mechanism. First, we design a Siamese Masked Autoencoder (SMAE) that learns transferable spatio-temporal representations for tracking by reconstructing masked regions in video sequences using template guidance. Second, we develop a Z-shaped Multi-directional Scanning Mamba (ZMamba) block to efficiently capture long-range dependencies and a Dynamic Relational Matcher (DRM) that establishes fine-grained, background-suppressed correlations between template and search regions. Additionally, a Spectral Attention Aggregation Module (SAAM) is introduced to adapt features to the hyperspectral domain. Extensive experiments on HOT2020 and HOT2023 benchmarks demonstrate that SiamMDR achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a precision of 94% and success rate of 67.6%, outperforming existing methods by a significant margin while effectively mitigating the domain shift and template noise problems. Published: 2026-06-05T16:11:49+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.782 (must_read)

Hyperspectral object tracking (HOT) faces significant challenges due to scarce data and the domain shift that arises when transferring RGB pre-trained models. To address these issues, we propose a novel two-stage framework SiamMDR, which introduces a task-specific pre-training strategy and a dynamic relational matching mechanism. First, we design a Siamese Masked Autoencoder (SMAE) that learns transferable spatio-temporal representations for tracking by reconstructing masked regions in video sequences using template guidance. Second, we develop a Z-shaped Multi-directional Scanning Mamba (ZMamba) block to efficiently capture long-range dependencies and a Dynamic Relational Matcher (DRM) that establishes fine-grained, background-suppressed correlations between template and search regions. Additionally, a Spectral Attention Aggregation Module (SAAM) is introduced to adapt features to the hyperspectral domain. Extensive experiments on HOT2020 and HOT2023 benchmarks demonstrate that SiamMDR achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a precision of 94% and success rate of 67.6%, outperforming existing methods by a significant margin while effectively mitigating the domain shift and template noise problems.

item.title: Embodied Spatial Affordance: Spatial-Aware Affordance Learning for Embodied Navigation and Manipulation
item.dc:creator: Xiaoshuai Hao
Yingbo Tang
Lingfeng Zhang
Long Chen
Wei Zhou
Jungong Han
Wenbo Ding
Xiao-Ping Zhang
item.description: Embodied navigation and manipulation are fundamental capabilities for embodied agents operating in physical environments. A key challenge in this process is understanding the spatial context and the affordances of the environment, which involves recognizing how objects can be interacted with (object affordance) and identifying suitable locations for movement and object placement (free space affordance). While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown promise in high-level task planning, their ability to translate reasoning into precise executable actions remains limited, particularly in image-based spatial understanding and precise affordance localization—a critical gap in image processing for robotics. To bridge this gap, we propose EspA, a novel image-to-keypoint model that leverages spatial-aware affordance learning to predict actionable affordances directly from 2D image inputs. Built on a hierarchical vision-language architecture, EspA jointly reasons about object affordances and free space affordances, enabling pixel-level localization of both types of interactions. Crucially, EspA translates language instructions into precise 2D affordance keypoints from observed images, which are then projected into 3D actionable coordinates using depth information. To support this unified affordance reasoning, we introduce the Embodied Spatial Affordance (ESA) dataset, which captures both object-centric interactions and free space contexts. By jointly modeling these affordances in a shared representation space, EspA overcomes the limitations of prior works that treat them independently. The dataset’s fine-grained annotations enable our model to learn the intricate relationship between object functionality and spatial feasibility, significantly enhancing the spatial understanding in embodied tasks. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that EspA outperforms existing state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models (VLMs), both open-source and closed-source, in object and free spa...
